ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for steamed minced pork with salted fish; FOODON_03315671 (pork dish) used as the closest broader class anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for this Singaporean Chinese dish. A0CKP (pork meat products) is used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor; steamed preparation noted.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular codes for traditional Chinese-Singaporean composite dishes.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of steamed minced pork with salted fish is approximately 150g, representing a single-person portion when served as one of several dishes in a shared Chinese meal.

nutrient_source.title: Steamed Minced Pork with Salted Fish — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for steamed minced pork with salted fish (cr_id F06100043). All per_100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 5g/100g based on typical pork fat composition from USDA SR Legacy (pork, ground, 80% lean / 20% fat, cooked) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this dish.

gi_source.title: N/A — negligible carbohydrate content
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glycemic index is not applicable for this dish. Carbohydrate content is 0.7g/100g, which is negligible and insufficient to produce a meaningful glycemic response. GI measurement is not meaningful for foods with less than 1g carbohydrate per 100g. GL per serving is effectively 0.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content at 793mg/100g, far exceeding the 600mg/100g threshold for hypertension concern
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish to very small portions (50g or less) on rare occasions, as a 150g serving delivers approximately 1,190mg sodium — nearly 80% of the 1,500mg daily sodium target recommended for hypertensive patients by the Singapore HPB.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (0.7g/100g) is glycaemically favourable, but high total fat (14.4g/100g) including estimated saturated fat (~5g/100g) and very high sodium (793mg/100g) present metabolic concerns
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is glycaemically safe for type 2 diabetes due to negligible carbohydrate content, but the high saturated fat content may worsen insulin resistance and the very high sodium load increases cardiovascular risk, which is already elevated in type 2 diabetes; limit portions to 100g and monitor overall dietary sodium intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combined burden of high sodium (793mg/100g), high total fat (14.4g/100g), and estimated saturated fat (~5g/100g) creates a significant cardiovascular risk profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish only occasionally and in small portions (50–80g), as the high sodium content raises blood pressure and the saturated fat content contributes to atherogenic lipid profiles; substituting salted fish with fresh fish would substantially reduce the sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium (793mg/100g) exceeds the 400mg/100g threshold associated with worsening RA-related tissue swelling, and pork contains arachidonic acid (omega-6) which amplifies inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish during flares due to the combination of very high sodium and red meat-derived arachidonic acid; if consumed, limit to 50–80g and pair with anti-inflammatory vegetables, or substitute salted fish with fresh fish to reduce sodium.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed minced pork with salted fish (咸鱼蒸猪肉饼, xian yu zheng zhu rou bing) is a classic Singaporean Chinese home-cooked dish and a staple of traditional Cantonese and Hokkien household cooking in Singapore. The dish is prepared by combining finely minced pork with small pieces of salted fish (typically haruan or threadfin), fresh ginger, and oyster sauce, then shaping the mixture into a flat patty and steaming it until cooked through. The result is a moist, tender, and intensely savoury patty with a pronounced umami depth from the salted fish and oyster sauce, complemented by the subtle warmth of ginger. The texture is soft and yielding, with occasional firm pieces of salted fish distributed throughout. Nutritionally, this dish is high in protein (20.5g/100g) and moderate in fat (14.4g/100g), with negligible carbohydrate (0.7g/100g), making it glycaemically neutral. However, the sodium content is extremely high at 793mg/100g due to the salted fish and oyster sauce, making it a significant concern for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. It is commonly served as one of several dishes in a shared Chinese meal, typically accompanied by steamed white rice. The dish is a frequent feature at breakfast and lunch in traditional Singaporean households and coffee shops (kopitiams). From a clinical nutrition perspective, the dish is suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns but requires careful portion control for sodium-sensitive individuals.

Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.7g/100g); gi_category set to not_applicable and glycemic_index set to null. GL per serving calculated as effectively 0.0 given negligible carbohydrate. The dish is not halal due to pork content. Oyster sauce may contain gluten depending on brand formulation.
